titleStmt.title: Letter from Basil Howe to the Earl of Aberdeen, 25 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Basil Howe

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter was sent by Basil Howe to John Campbell Gordon. In this letter Basil offers his services in any capacity upon hearing of a Rising in Dublin. Basil notes his previous experience and believes he has an intimate knowledge of Ireland and the Irish peopleJohn Campbell Hamilton-Gordon, 1st Marquess of Aberdeen and Temair (1847-1934), known as The Earl of Aberdeen from 1870 to 1916, was a Scottish politician. Gordon served twice as Lord Lieutenant of Ireland.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/7677

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
COPLEY, BEDFORD. April 25th/16. To His Excellency The Earl of Aberdeen Dublin - Sir: I have read with great
indignation of the Rebel Rising
in Dublin. I have the honour to be
one of Your Excellencies, Justices
of the Peace for Dublin and although
it is seven years since I left
Ireland, it is my loyal duty and
pleasure, to offer to return to Ireland
- at once, in any capacity in which
Your Excellency, may find my use. My knowledge of Ireland
and its people, in all parts of the
country is intimate. For seven
Years I was Asst: Mech: Engineer
to the M.G.W. Ry, of Ireland, having
control of large bodies of men and travelled with his late Majesty
King Edward round Ireland and in a
Royal Train which we built at
Broadstone. Later with the
Renard Train, which I showed
Your Excellency, I went all round
Ireland, in the interest of Transport.
As such - Board of Works Inspectors
are all familiar to me. Further I wish to come to
your service in Dublin, as I
remember the debt of gratitude
to your House, for the kindness
shown to my cousin, W. E. Hope
Your Excellencius R.D.C. of the Irish
Guards, who laid down his Life at
Zillebeche. At present I am
doing what I can as an Inspecting
Engineer at The Royal Arsenal
Woolwich - but I am ready to
take up service under Your Excellency
and as being an Inspector of Dublin. Sir: Your obedient servant Basil Howe.
